behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure grouping algorithm with no external I/O, OS, or ML dependency. The failure boundary is whether the grouping logic correctly keys by trace ID. A unit test passing test log entries (with various trace IDs) through the grouping function and verifying the output keying is correct will catch failures in the algorithm. Mocks are not needed—just structured test data. The algorithm either correctly groups by trace ID or it doesn't.
behaviors.test_description: Create test log entries from 3 different microservices with 2 matching trace IDs and 1 unique. Pass through grouping function. Assert: exactly 2 groups; group 1 contains 2 entries; group 2 contains 1 entry; all entries in group 1 have the same trace ID.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering algorithm applied to timestamps with potential skew. No real clock source, network, or external dependency is needed. The failure is: timestamps don't sort correctly when clock skew is present. A unit test with mock timestamps (including values separated by <500ms to simulate skew) will verify the ordering logic. The test either passes (correct handling) or fails (skew not handled). No integration is required.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5 log entries with timestamps: T, T+100ms, T+400ms, T+600ms (from service with 200ms clock offset), T+50ms. Feed to timeline ordering function. Assert order is: T, T+50ms, T+100ms, T+400ms, T+600ms (compensating for skew or using consistent ordering strategy).

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: This is a statistical detection algorithm. The failure is: spike detection doesn't trigger when p99 exceeds 3x baseline. A unit test with mock latency arrays (baseline values + one spike value) will verify the calculation and threshold logic. No real metrics pipeline or external data source is required. The algorithm either detects the spike or it doesn't.
behaviors.test_description: Create latency array with baseline of ~100ms (values: 90, 95, 100, 105, 110 for p99 calculation) plus spike value of 350ms. Feed to anomaly detector. Assert: spike is flagged; anomaly report includes p99 value and 3x threshold comparison.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: While individual JSON parsing could be unit-tested, the real failure is pipeline-level: does the system crash or lose the skipped count when processing a batch containing malformed entries? A mock JSON parser won't reveal if the pipeline crashes downstream or if the counter is incremented correctly across multiple components. MOCK-MISS: mocking parse() success/failure hides the interaction failure (pipeline didn't wrap parsing in exception handling, counter not incremented atomically, etc.). Need integration: real malformed JSON fed through the pipeline with verification that (a) no exception propagates, (b) skipped count matches number of malformed entries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a batch of 100 log entries: 95 valid JSON, 5 malformed (truncated, invalid syntax). Feed batch through ingestion pipeline. Assert: pipeline completes without crash; anomaly report shows skipped_count=5; valid entries are processed; final timeline contains exactly 95 entries.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: This is a non-functional requirement (throughput/performance) dependent on real OS behavior, I/O latency, threading/async scheduling, and resource constraints. MOCK-MISS: mocking I/O as instant would hide buffer overflow, blocking, or resource starvation bugs. A pure integration test with in-memory structures won't reveal whether real disk I/O, network I/O, or database writes become a bottleneck. Real failure modes include: queues fill and block, entries dropped due to buffer limits, or the system falls behind cursor. Must stress-test with realistic I/O characteristics (real files, network, or simulated with realistic latency). Only a system-level stress test reveals if the system can sustain 10k/s without resource exhaustion.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy tool in test environment. Use log producer to emit 10,000 entries/second for 30 seconds (300k total). Entries span 5 services with realistic I/O (write to local file or in-process queue with bounded latency). Assert: final entry count matches 300k; no entries dropped; max processing latency does not exceed 5 seconds (tool not falling behind); anomaly detection completes within SLA.
